## Science and Philosophy

![Science and Philosophy](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:76:76/1*hMtEV040oAti2lShjKDTcA.jpeg)

Medium’s center for scientifically-informed content.

Member-only story

# The History & Philosophy Of Science: More Important Than Ever

![Rory Cockshaw](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*QWWqVUnRMcyapGjCFcKLqg.png)

--

Share

![]()

If you believe the doomsayers, society is falling apart. We’re living in an age of tension between maskers and anti-maskers; climate activists and climate deniers; flat-Earthers (a minority, to be sure, but a vocal one) and round-Earthers. Nuclear weapons exist, and they’re widespread; we might be teetering on the brink of the most technologically advanced (read: “destructive”) warfare in history.

All of these issues — every single one of them — is fundamentally scientific in nature. Every piece of data, every invention, every step forwards or backwards, was ultimately made by some (often nameless) scientist.

The public’s relationship with science has been fraught since Galileo tried to convince everyone that the Sun was in the centre of the Solar System in the early 17th century. Before that, even: Socrates (a philosopher rather than a scientist, but was not an old name for science ‘Natural Philosophy’?) made such a name for himself by pulling the philosophical and religious rug from beneath the lay-person’s feet, that he was finally condemned to death.

Over 2000 years later, and little has changed but extent. We still don’t like being told what to do by ‘shrinks’ in lab-coats. Adverse reactions to masks, social distancing, and self-isolation are nothing new. The public wants to know…

--

--

![Science and Philosophy](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:96:96/1*hMtEV040oAti2lShjKDTcA.jpeg)
![Science and Philosophy](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:128:128/1*hMtEV040oAti2lShjKDTcA.jpeg)

The history of science covers developments in the natural and social sciences and mathematics from antiquity to the modern day.

FULL CONTENT:
![Banner](//d2jv02qf7xgjwx.cloudfront.net/sites/3767/banner/University_of_Notre_Dame.jpg)

# History and Philosophy of Science; and Science, Technology, and Values

## Librarian

![Profile Photo](//d2jv02qf7xgjwx.cloudfront.net/accounts/393158/profiles/403130/Carolyn_Scearce2.jpg)

## Overview of History and Philosophy of Science

[![John J. Reilly Center](https://libapps.s3.amazonaws.com/accounts/44519/images/ReillyCenter.jpg)](https://reilly.nd.edu/)

![John J. Reilly Center](https://libapps.s3.amazonaws.com/accounts/44519/images/ReillyCenter.jpg)

The history of science covers developments in the natural and social sciences and mathematics from antiquity to the modern day. The philosophy of science covers what science is, how it works, and the logic by which we build scientific knowledge. Both fields are inter-, cross-, and multi-disciplinary.

Society, technology, and values inspires student reflection on connections between and across disciplines, examining science, technology, and medicine through the lens of the liberal arts and in the service of human dignity and the common good.

Related guides include [History, Western Europe](https://libguides.library.nd.edu/history-western-europe); [Philosophy](https://libguides.library.nd.edu/philosophy); [Theology and Religion](https://libguides.library.nd.edu/theology-and-religion); [Leisure Reading — Science and Technology](https://libguides.library.nd.edu/reading-science-technology); in addition to the various guides for the sciences.

Related topics include: [Your Scholarly Identity (ORCID)](https://libguides.library.nd.edu/scholarly-identity), [Research Data Services](https://libguides.library.nd.edu/research-data-services), [Scholarly Communication](https://libguides.library.nd.edu/scholarly-communication/), [Open Access Publishing at Notre Dame](https://libguides.library.nd.edu/open-access-publishing-notredame).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Academic discipline

[![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/99/Question_book-new.svg/60px-Question_book-new.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg)This article **needs additional citations for [verification](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Special:EditPage/History_and_philosophy_of_science "Special:EditPage/History and philosophy of science") by [adding citations to reliable sources](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed.

_Find sources:_["History and philosophy of science"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22History+and+philosophy+of+science%22)–[news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22History+and+philosophy+of+science%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1)**·**[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22History+and+philosophy+of+science%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks)**·**[books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22History+and+philosophy+of+science%22+-wikipedia)**·**[scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22History+and+philosophy+of+science%22)**·**[JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22History+and+philosophy+of+science%22&acc=on&wc=on)_(June 2007)_ _([Learn how and when to remove this message](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:Maintenance\_template\_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))_

The **history and philosophy of science** (**HPS**) is an [academic discipline](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Discipline_(academia) "Discipline (academia)") that encompasses the [philosophy of science](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philosophy_of_science "Philosophy of science") and the [history of science](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_science "History of science"). Although many scholars in the field are trained primarily as either historians or as philosophers, there are degree-granting departments of HPS at several prominent universities. Though philosophy of science and history of science are their own disciplines, history and philosophy of science is a discipline in its own right.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_and_philosophy_of_science#cite_note-galison-1)[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_and_philosophy_of_science#cite_note-weingart-2)

Philosophy of science is a branch of philosophy concerned with the foundations, [methods](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_method "Scientific method"), and implications of [science](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Science "Science"). The central questions of this study concern what qualifies as science, the reliability of [scientific theories](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_theories "Scientific theories"), and the ultimate purpose of science. This discipline overlaps with metaphysics/[ontology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ontology "Ontology") and [epistemology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epistemology "Epistemology"), for example, when it explores the relationship between science and truth. Philosophy of science focuses on metaphysical, epistemic and semantic aspects of science. Ethical issues such as [bioethics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bioethics "Bioethics") and [scientific misconduct](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_misconduct "Scientific misconduct") are often considered ethics or [science studies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Science_studies "Science studies") rather than philosophy of science.

There is no consensus among philosophers about many of the central problems concerned with the philosophy of science, including whether science can reveal the [truth about unobservable things](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_realism "Scientific realism") and [whether scientific reasoning can be justified](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Problem_of_induction "Problem of induction") at all. In addition to these general questions about science as a whole, philosophers of science consider problems that apply to particular sciences (such as [astronomy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philosophy_of_science#Philosophy_of_astronomy "Philosophy of science"), [biology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philosophy_of_biology "Philosophy of biology"), [chemistry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philosophy_of_chemistry "Philosophy of chemistry"), [Earth science](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philosophy_of_science#Philosophy_of_Earth_sciences "Philosophy of science"), or [physics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philosophy_of_physics "Philosophy of physics")). Some philosophers of science also use contemporary results in science to reach conclusions about philosophy itself.

History
-------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=History_and_philosophy_of_science&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

One origin of the unified discipline is the historical approach to the discipline of the philosophy of science. This hybrid approach is reflected in the career of [Thomas Kuhn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thomas_Kuhn "Thomas Kuhn"). His first permanent appointment, at the [University of California, Berkeley](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley"),[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_and_philosophy_of_science#cite_note-3) was to a position advertised by the philosophy department, but he also taught courses from the history department. When he was promoted to full professor in the history department only, Kuhn was offended at the philosophers' rejection because "I sure as hell wanted to be there, and it was my philosophy students who were working with me, not on philosophy but on history, were nevertheless my more important students".[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_and_philosophy_of_science#cite_note-4) This attitude is also reflected in his historicist approach, as outlined in Kuhn's seminal [Structure of Scientific Revolutions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Structure_of_Scientific_Revolutions "Structure of Scientific Revolutions") (1962, 2nd ed. 1970), wherein philosophical questions about scientific theories and, especially, theory change are understood in historical terms, employing concepts such as [paradigm shift](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paradigm_shift "Paradigm shift").

However, Kuhn was also critical of attempts fully to unify the _methods_ of history and philosophy of science: "Subversion is not, I think, too strong a term for the likely result of an attempt to make the two fields into one. They differ in a number of their central constitutive characteristics, of which the most general and apparent is their goals. The final product of most historical research is a narrative, a story, about particulars of the past. [...] The philosopher, on the other hand, aims principally at explicit generalizations and at those with universal scope. He is no teller of stories, true or false. His goal is to discover and state what is true at all times and places rather than to impart understanding of what occurred at a particular time and place."[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_and_philosophy_of_science#cite_note-5) More recent work questions whether these methodological and conceptual divisions are in fact barriers to a unified discipline.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_and_philosophy_of_science#cite_note-6)

Precursors, Seminal Works & Popular Standards
---------------------------------------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=History_and_philosophy_of_science&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Precursors, Seminal Works & Popular Standards")]

Precursors works in this field include [The Origin of German Tragic Drama](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Origin_of_German_Tragic_Drama "The Origin of German Tragic Drama") by [Walter Benjamin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Walter_Benjamin "Walter Benjamin"), [Being and Time](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Being_and_Time "Being and Time") by [Martin Heidegger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Martin_Heidegger "Martin Heidegger") and various other works by these two. Marshall McLuhan and Foucault are precursors whose continued work was adjacent to and overlapping with domain. Publications by the [Warburg Institute](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warburg_Institute "Warburg Institute") including monographs, studies and its journals make up much of the precursor material that the discipline was built up from (see especially: [Frances Yates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frances_Yates "Frances Yates"), [D.P. Walker](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/D._P._Walker "D. P. Walker"), [Aby Warburg](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aby_Warburg "Aby Warburg") himself, [Fritz Saxl](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fritz_Saxl "Fritz Saxl"), [Erwin Panofsky](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Erwin_Panofsky "Erwin Panofsky") etc. [Thomas Kuhn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thomas_Kuhn "Thomas Kuhn") himself published his first major papers and studies via the institute—he might be called a popularizer or legitimsting figure in a field that was especially arcane in its concerns before the widespread success and influence of his major book). Proceedings and papers presented at the [Eranos Conference](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eranos "Eranos") hosted by the [Bollingen Foundation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bollingen_Foundation "Bollingen Foundation") as a legacy of the estate of [Carl Jung](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carl_Jung "Carl Jung") form both precursors and early exemplars of the seminal work.

--- SOURCE 5: What's the point of history of science? ---
URL: https://www.reddit.com/r/PhilosophyofScience/comments/1fb1ch7/whats_the_point_of_history_of_science/

SUMMARY:
Learning about the early history of science for no reason other than to know about it is the most fundamental spirit of science.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: The history and philosophy of science examines the evolution of scientific knowledge, emphasizing its foundations, methodologies, and impact on society, shaping our understanding of the world.

This field intersects with various disciplines, including history, philosophy, sociology, and technology, enhancing our comprehension of science's role within broader intellectual and cultural contexts.
Scientific thought has transformed over centuries, influenced by paradigm shifts, cultural contexts, and technological advancements, reflecting humanity's quest for knowledge and understanding.
Critical themes include the nature of scientific inquiry, ethics, the relationship between science and society, and the philosophical implications of scientific discoveries and theories.
